for they cherish designs of plunder, and mischief is on their lips.
By wisdom a house is built up, by intelligence it is established;
by knowledge its shambers are filled with all precious and pleasant substance.
Wise men are better than strong men, and knowledge is better than might;
for wars are waged by wise guidance. and victory lies in counsellors.
Wisdom is too high for a fool; so he opens not his mouth in the gate.
The man who deviseth mischief is known among men as a schemer.
The scheming of fools is sin, and the scoffer is hated of men.
If you have been slack, in the day of distress your resource will be scanty.
Rescue those that are taken to death; save those that are tottering to slaughter.
If you say, "It is not in my power," he who weighs the heart, he discerns; He who watches your soul, he knows, and on each he will bring back his deeds.
As the honey you eat, my son, is wholesome. and sweet to your taste is the honeycomb,
even so, be assured, to your soul is wisdom.
Lie not in wait for the home of the just, and do not assail his dwelling-place;
for the just, though he fall seven times, will rise, but the wicked will stumble to ruin.
At the fall of your foe rejoice not, and do not exult at his overthrow;
lest the Lord be displeased when he sees it, and turn His anger away from him.
Be not fretful because of evildoers. nor envious of the wicked;
for the bad man will have no future. The lamp of the wicked is quenched.
Fear the Lord, my son, and the king, be not haughty to one or the other;
for swift is the ruin they raise, the disaster they send unforeseen. Appendix to the Second Collection of Proverbs
These also belong to the wise men: It is wrong to be partial in giving of judgment.
All men will curse and all people will execrate him who pronounces the guilty man innocent.
But those whose decisions are just will fare pleasantly; blessings of fortune will rest upon them.
As one who kisses the lips is he who returns a straight answer.
Set your business in order without, make all ready in your fields: after that you may build up your house.
Bear not false witness against your neighbour, nor let your lips be deceitful.
Do not threaten to treat him as he treated you, and to visit his deed with retribution. On the Sluggard
By the field of the sluggard I passed. by the vineyard of one that was foolish.
It was all overgrown with thistles; its surface was covered with nettles; its wall of stone was in ruins.
As I looked, I thought upon it, and I drew from the sight a lesson.
"Just a little more sleep, a little more slumber, a little more lying with folded hands."
So will poverty come upon you like a robber, and want like an armed man.
These also are proverbs of Solomon, which the men of Hezekiah, king of Judah, copied out.
The glory of God is his mystery, but the glory of kings is investigation.
Like the height of the heavens and the depth of the earth, so the purpose of kings is unsearchable.
Remove the dross from silver, and forth it comes pure altogether.
Remove wicked men from the king. and his throne is established by righteousness.
In the presence of a king claim not honour, nor stand in the place of the great.
Better be told, "Come up hither," than be humbled before the prince.
Do not hastily bring up a law-suit of something your eyes have seen. For what will you do in the end, when your neighbour has put you to shame?
Discuss the affair with your neighbour, reveal not his secret to others;
lest those that have heard it reproach you and your infamy pass not away.
Like apples of gold in carvings of silver is a word that is fittingly spoken.
Like an earring of gold and a necklace of fine gold is a wise man's reproof to a listening ear.
Like a drink cooled by snow in the time of harvest is a faithful envoy to those that send him: he refreshes the soul of his master.
As clouds and wind that yet bring no rain, so is one that boasts of gifts that he gives not.
An angry man can be won by forbearance, and bones can be broken by gentle words.
If you find honey, eat just what you need. lest, after a surfeit, you vomit it up.
In the house of your friend let your foot be but seldom, lest, sated with you, he detest you.
One who bears false witness against his neighbour is a hammer, a sword, and a sharp-pointed arrow.
In a crumbling tooth and a foot unsteady is the faithless man's trust in the day of distress.
As vinegar to a wound is a song to a sorrowful heart.
Give your enemy food, if he hunger; and water, if he be thirsty:
for so will you heap coals of fire on his head, and the Lord will give you recompense.
As the north wind brings forth rain, so slander an angry countenance.
It is better to live on the roof in a corner than in a spacious house with a quarrelsome woman.
Like cool water to one that is weary is good news from a distant land.
Like a fountain befouled or a ruined spring is a just man who falls before the wicked.
It is not good to eat much honey; be sparing then of your compliments.
Like a city whose walls are broken down is a man without self-control.
In a fool is honour as unbecoming as snow in summer or rain in harvest.
Like the aimless flight of a sparrow or swallow, the curse that is baseless does not come home.
A whip for the horse, a bridle for the ass, and a rod for the back of fools.
Do not answer a fool as beseems his folly, in case you, too, become like him.
Answer a fool as beseems his folly, lest he fancy himself to be wise.
To send a fool with a message is to cut off one's feet and to drink disaster.
Like the limp legs of the lame is a proverb in the mouth of a fool.
Like a bundle of jewels on a heap of stones is honour conferred on a fool.
Like a thorn-stick brandished by a drunken man is a proverb in the mouth of a fool.
All fools must suffer sore anguish of body. and their insolence will be shattered.
Like a dog that returns to his vomit, a fool repeats his folly.
If you see a man who thinks himself wise, there is far more hope for a fool than for him. On the Sluggard
The lazy man saith, "There's a lion on the road, there's a lion on the street."
As the door turns on its hinges, so the lazy man in his bed.
The lazy man buries his hand in the dish - too weary to carry it back to his mouth.
The lazy man thinks himself wiser than seven who can answer discreetly.
To mix in a quarrel not your own is to catch a dog by the ears.
Like a madman who hurls about deadly fire-brands and arrows,
is the man who deceives his neighbour and says that he did it in jest.
In the absence of wood the fire goes out. In the absence of slander contention ceases.
As charcoal to embers or wood to fire is a quarrelsome man for kindling strife.
Slanderous words are like dainty morsels, down they glide to the innermost being. On Hypocrisy
Like a sherd overlaid with silver slag is a wicked heart with glowing lips.
With his lips one who hates you dissembles. but he cherishes guile in his heart.
When he speaks you fair, trust him not: in his heart lurk hateful things seven.
He who digs a pit will fall into it; he who rolls a stone gets it back upon him,
A false tongue brings destruction; a flattering mouth works ruin.
Boast not yourself of tomorrow, for you do not know what a day may bring forth.
Let another mouth praise you, not yours - the lips of some other, not yours.
A stone is heavy and sand is weighty, but heavier than both the vexation caused by fools.
Indignation is fierce, anger pours like a flood; but who can stand before jealousy?
Better an open reproof than affection that is concealed.
The wounds of a friend are sincere, but profuse are the kisses of a foe.
Honey is scorned by a man that is full, but anything bitter is sweet to the hungry.
Like a bird that strays from its nest is a man that wanders from home.
Oil and perfume rejoice the heart, but sorrow of spirit does rend it asunder.
Your friend and your father's friend forsake not; and in your day of distress do not enter the house of your brother, near neighbour is better than distant brother.
Be wise, my son, and gladden my heart. that so I may answer the man who would taunt me.
The prudent scent mischief and hide; the simple go on and are punished.
Take the garment of him who goes bail for another, and hold it in pledge for that other.
If early in the morning one loudly blesses another, it is reckoned to him as a curse.
An endless drip on a rainy day and a quarrelsome wife are alike;
he hides the wind that would hide her, and his hand grasps at oil.
As iron sharpens iron, so a man sharpens his friend.
He that tends a fig tree will eat its fruit; so one who attends to his lord will get honour.
As face answers to face so the mind of one man to another.
Abaddon and Sheol are ever unsated. and ever unsated the eyes of men.
As the smelter for silver, the furnace for gold, so repute is the test of a man.
Though you bray a fool with a pestle along with bruised grain in a mortar. you will not get him rid of his folly. Advice to Farmers
Look well to the state of your flock, and give your mind to your herds;
For wealth lasts not forever, nor riches through all generations.
When the hay is removed and the new growth appears, and the grass of the uplands is all gathered in,
Then the lambs will supply you with clothing, and the goats with the price of a field;
goats' milk enough there will be for your food, and a livelihood for your slave-girls.
The wicked flee, when no man pursues, but the righteous are bold as a lion.
By the sin of the violent quarrels arise, but a man of sense will extinguish them.
A wicked man that oppresses the poor is a deluging rain that leaves no food.
Those who turn from instruction admire the wicked, but those who observe it are zealous against them.
The wicked have no understanding of justice, but who cares for the Lord understands it completely.
Better a poor man whose life is blameless than one who is crooked, although he be rich.
A son that is prudent observes instruction, but the comrade of profligates shames his father.
He that adds to his substance by interest or increase but gathers for him that is kind to the poor.
If one turns a deaf ear to instruction, his very prayers are detestable.
He that turns the upright to wicked ways will himself fall into the pit that he dug; but the blameless will come to prosperity.
A rich man may think himself wise, but a poor man with brains can see through him.
What a pageant there is, when the just are triumphant! When the wicked emerge into power, men hide.
No man will prosper that covers his sins, but those that confess and forsake them find mercy.
Happy the man who fears always, but the obstinate plunge to disaster.
A roaring lion, a prowling bear, is a bad man who rules an impoverished people,
That prince has no prudence that plays the oppressor. but long will he live that detests unjust gain.
He who sheds the blood of a man - let him flee to a city, let none apprehend him.
The blameless life will be kept in safety, but the crooked life will suddenly fall.
He that tills his land will have plenty of bread. but idle pursuits end in plenty of poverty.
A trustworthy man will be richly blessed, but who hastes to get riches will not go unpunished.
It is wrong for a man to be partial, to sin for a piece of bread.
A greedy man hastes to be rich, not knowing that want will befall him.
A man who reproves gets more thanks in the end than a smooth-tongued flatterer.
One who robs his parents and says, "It is no sin," is companion to him who destroys.
A greedy man stirs up strife; he who trusts in the Lord will flourish.
He who trusts in himself is a fool; he who walks in wisdom is safe.
He who gives to the poor will not come to want, but who veileth his eyes will have many a curse.
When the wicked emerge into power, men hide; when they perish, the righteous increase.
One who stiffens his neck against all reproof will be suddenly crushed beyond healing.
When the just are in power, the people are glad; when the wicked hold sway, the people groan.
A man who loves wisdom brings joy to his father, but the comrade of harlots consumes his substance.
By justice a king sets his country erect, but one whose exactions are heavy destroys it.
A man who cajols his neighbour is spreading a net for his steps.
By his sin is a wicked man snared, but the righteous does shout for joy.
To the righteous the cause of the poor is dear, but the wicked care nothing at all.
Scoffers inflame a city (with discord), but wise men turn passions aside.
When a wise man disputes with a fool, he storms and laughs, and peace there is none.
Men of blood hate the blameless; the wicked seek for his life.
The fool lets his temper go, but a wise man restrains his anger.
If a ruler pays heed to false tales, his officials all grow to be scoundrels.
Oppressor and poor meet together, but the light in the eyes of them both is the Lord's.
If a king be faithful and just to the poor, his throne will stand forever.
The rod of correction brings wisdom, but a child let loose brings disgrace on his mother.
When the wicked hold sway, wrong increases, but the righteous will gloat on their downfall.
Correct your son and your mind will be eased, and he will delight your soul.
People break loose in the absence of vision, but he who observes instruction is happy.
Not by words can a servant be trained; for he knows, but he will not obey.
See you a man of hasty speech, there is far more hope for a fool than for him.
The spoiled child will end as a servant, and come to grief at the last.
A passionate man stirs up strife, and much evil is wrought by hot temper.
Pride will lay a man low, but the lowly attain to honour.
His own foe is he who goes shares with a thief; he hears the curse, but he utters nothing.
The fear of man brings a snare, but who trusts the Lord is safe.
Many seek royal favour, but it is God who decides every man's fate.
The righteous abhor the unjust, and the wicked abhor the upright.
The words of Agur, son of Jakeh, the Massaite. Oracle of the man. The Weary World-Problem I have wearied myself, O God; O God, I am weary and spent:
For dull as a brute am I, not a man with the mind of a man.
I have not learned wisdom, and nothing I know of the Holy One.

Who has climbed the heavens and come down? Who has gathered the wind in his fist? Who has tied in a garment the waters, or set up the bounds of the earth? What is his name, or his son's name? for surely you know.
The words of God have all been tested, he shields those who take refuge in him.
To his words add you nothing at all, lest He should convict you of being a liar. A Prayer for Preservation alike from Wealth and Poverty
For two things I entreat you; deny me not, before I die.
Put falseness and lying away from me; give me neither riches nor poverty, grant me the food I need;
lest, if surfeited, I deny you, and say, "Who then is the Lord?" Or poverty drive me to steal, and profane the name of my God. Against Defamation
To a master defame not his servant, lest he curse you and you have to smart for it. Four Evil Types
There are those who curse their fathers, and leave their mothers unblessed.
There are others that think themselves pure, yet are all unwashed of their filthiness.
There are others with haughty eyes and supercilious eyebrows.
There are others whose teeth are swords - the teeth in their jaws are knives, to devour the poor from the earth. and the needy from off the ground. Four Insatiable Things
There are three things that never are satisfied - four that say never, "Enough!"
Sheol; the womb that is barren; the earth unsated with water; and fire that says never, "Enough!" Against Contempt of Parents
The eye that mocks a father, and scornes an aged mother, will be picked by the crows of the valley and clean devoured by vultures. Four Mysterious Things
Three things are too wonderful for me - four are beyond my knowledge:
the way of a vulture in air, the way of a snake on a rock, the way of a ship on the sea, and the way of a man with a woman. Four Intolerable Things
Under three things the earth does tremble - four she cannot bear:
A slave when he comes to the throne, a fool who has more than enough,
a plain woman when she gets married, a maid that is heir to her mistress. Four Things Little but Wise
Of the small things of earth there are four. and wiser they are than the wisest.
The ants are a feeble folk, yet they lay up their food in the summer.
A feeble folk, too, are the conies, yet they make their house in the rocks.
The locusts again, though kingless, yet march, every one, in good order.
And a lizard you could crush in your hand finds her way into royal palaces. Four Stately Things
Three creatures there are whose step is stately, four whose step is majestic -
The lion, most valiant of beasts, who in presence of foe never flinches;
The proud strutting cock and the he-goat, and a king who is head of his army.
Bluster you not in arrogance, but lay your hand on your mouth.
As the churning of milk yields curd, and the wringing of the nose yields blood, so the churning of wrath yields strife.
The words of Lemuel, king of Massa, which his mother taught him.
O son whom I bore, give heed to my words; and observe my sayings, you son of my vows.
Give not your strength to women, nor your love to those that slay kings.
Nor for kings is it right to drink wine, or for princes to long for strong drink;
Lest in drink they forget the law, and do wrong to the cause of the sorrowful.
But give drink to him who is perishing, and wine to the bitter in soul;
so that in drink he forget his poverty, and think of his sorrow no more.
Open your mouth for the widow, do right by all fatherless children.
Open your mouth in just judgment. defend the poor and the needy. The Ideal Housewife
A woman of worth who can find? Her price is far above corals.
To her her husband trusts, and finds no lack of gain.
She does him good and not harm all the days of his life.
She looks out wool and flax. and works it up as she will.
Like the merchant-ships is she; she brings her food from afar.
She rises while yet it is night, and gives her household food, and her maidens their portion appointed.
She examines a field and buys it; with her earnings she plants a vineyard,
She girds her loins with strength, and with vigour she plies her arms.
She perceives that her profit is good; her lamp never goes out in the night.
She lays her hand on the distaff; her hand takes hold of the spindle.
She stretches her hand to the poor, and her hand she extends to the needy.
She fears not the snow for her household; her household are all clad in scarlet.
And coverlets she has made her; her raiment is linen and purple.
In the gates is her husband well known, where he sits with the elders in council.
Linen she makes and sells; to the merchant she furnishes girdles.
She is clothed with strength and glory; she laughs at the days to come.
Her mouth she opens in wisdom; kind counsel is on her tongue.
She looks well to the ways of her household; she eats not the bread of idleness.
Her children arise and bless her; her husband sings her praises:
"Many daughters have done nobly, but you excellest them all!"
Grace is deceptive, and beauty is transient. but a woman of character - she will be praised.
Give her then what her hands have earned, even the praise of her deeds in the gates.

books.name: Ecclesiastes
chapters: Discourses, by the Speaker, the son of David, king of Jerusalem. The Futility and Monotomy of Nature and Of Human Life
Utterest vanity! The Speaker declareth: Utterest vanity! All is vanity.
What gain hath a man of all his toil Whereat he toileth under the sun?
The generations come and go, But evermore the earth abideth.
The sun doth rise, and the sun doth set, But he panteth back to the place of his rising.
South the wind goeth, and northward it circleth; Circling and circling goeth the wind, And back on its circling the wind returneth.
All the rivers run into the sea, But nevertheless is the sea not full. To the place to which the rivers run, Thither they run and run for ever.
All things are full or weariness, Of weariness unutterable, With all that it sees hath the eye no rest, And with all that it hears is the ear unfilled. The Futility of the Search after Knowledge
What has been, shall be; what has happened already, will happen again: there is not a novelty
under the sun. When anything occurs that one is disposed to call really new, it will be found to have
happened already - ages before us. Nobody remembers (to-day) the people of the olden time, and similarly the people of the after-time will not be remebered by anybody who comes after them.
I, the Speaker, was king over Israel in Jerusalem;
and I gave my mind to the philosophic study and investigation of all that goes on under the sun. But a sorry business it is that God has given men to
busy themselves with. From my observation of all that goes on under the sun, I have come to the conclusion that it is all nothing but an illusion and a chasing of the wind.
That which is crooked can never be straightened, And that which is lacking can never be counted.
Then I said to myself, Let me take my own case. I have amassed wisdom beyond all my predecessors in Jerusalem, and my experience of wisdom and of
knowledge has been a wide one; but after applying my mind to the study of wisdom and knowledge, madness and folly, I am convinced that this also is a chasing of the wind; for
Who is rich in wisdom is rich in vexation, And increase of knowledge brings increase of pain. The Futility of the Search after Pleasure
I said to myself, Well now, I will experiment with pleasure and indulge myself; but I discovered with
Surprise that this, too, was an illusion. I concluded that laughter was madness and joy a sterile thing.

I turned over in my mind how to cheer my senses with wine - preserving at the same time, however, my habitual wisdom - and how to embrace folly, until I should discover what satisfaction may be procured by men under heaven during the days
of their brief lives. I went in for enterprises on an impressive scale. I had houses built and vineyards
planted. I had gardens laid out and parks planted
with all sorts of fruit trees, I had reservoirs con- structed to water the trees that formed the plan-
tations. I bought male and female slaves in addition to the other that had been born in my house. I had cattle and sheep in abundance - far beyond my
predecessors in Jerusalem. Further, I amassed silver and gold and treasure from (tributary) kings and from the provinces. I procured male and female singers and sensuous delighs - concubines in
abundance; and richer and richer I grew beyond all my predecessors in Jerusalem - taking care, how-

ever, to retain my wisdom. I refused my eyes nothing that they longed for, and I did not abstain from pleasure of any kind, for there was a pleasure attached to all my effort, and the reward of all my

effort I found in that. But when I looked at all the things my hands had made, and at the effort that I had spent upon them, it all turned out to be nothing but an illusion and chasing of the wind. There was no profit under the sun. The Futility of Wisdom
Then I turned to the consideration of wisdom and madness and folly,
and I saw that wisdom is as superior to folly as light to darkness; for
While the wise have their eyes in their head, The fool walketh in darkness. Still, I am well aware that in their fate they are
both alike. So I said to myself, The fate of the fool shall be my fate also; and what, in that case, am I the better for my pre-eminent wisdom? So I

said to myself, Here is another illusion. For through all time the wise man is not remembered any more than the fool, seeing that in the days to come every one will be soon forgotten. Alas! the wise man
dies just like the fool. So life became odious to me, because I was vexed with all that goes on under the sun; for it is all an illusion and a chasing of the wind, The Futility of Work
Yes, all the effort that I had spent under the sun became odious to me, because I should have to
leave it to my successor; and who can tell whether it will be a wise man or a fool that will have the disposal of the results of all my wise and earnest toil under the sun? Here is another illusion.
Then I felt like yielding to despair because

of all my laborious toil under the sun; for it may happen that a man who has toiled with wisdom, knowledge, and skill has to bequeath the results of it to another who has done no work upon it at all. Here, in this great evil, is another illusion.
For what does a man get from all the striving
and the strain of his work under the sun? His days are all a torture, and his business a vexation: why, even the night brings no rest to his mind. Here is
another illusion. There is, then, no satisfaction for a man beyond eating and drinking and enjoying himself as he works. This I saw to be from God's
own hand; for how can there be eating or enjoyment apart form Him?

For the man who pleases Him He gives wisdom, knowledge and happiness; to the sinner, on the other hand, He gives the task of gathering and amassing, that it may be given (in the end) to the man who pleases God. Here is another illusion and chasing of the wind. The Futility of Human Effort in the Light of the Fixed Order of the World
Everything has its reason appointed, And every affair under heaven has its time.
A time to be born, And a time to die. A time to plant, And a time to uproot.
A time to slay, And a time to heal. A time to tear down, And a time to build up.
A time to weep, And a time to laugh. A time to mourn, And a time to dance.
A time to scatter stones, And a time to clear them away. A time to embrace, And a time to refrain.
A time to seek, And a time to lose. A time to preserve, And a time to throw away.
A time to tear, And a time to sew. A time to be silent, And a time to speak.
A time to love, And a time to hate. A time for war, And a time for peace.
What does the worker gain by all his toil (thought I) as I looked at the tasks that God has assigned to men to busy themselves with?

It is a beautiful orfer that He has established- evrything at its appointed time. Besides, He has planted in the human heart (the instinct for) eternity; only men cannot discern the whole range - from begin- ning to end - of the work which God is carrying on.
I am convinced that the only satisfaction that can be theirs is to be happy and prosperous, while
they live. Besides, it is God's own gift, when a man is priviledged to eat, drink, and experience happiness in all his work.
I am convinced that all that God does is eternal; it is capable neither of increase nor of diminution: and God has ordained this, in order to inspire men with reverence.

Whatever is, has happened already; and what is yet to happen, already is: for that which has drifted (into the past) God seeketh out again. The Futility of Hoping for the Redress of Injustice in Some Future World

Once more, in the course of my observation under the sun, I saw that, in the place where judgments were delivered, there was injustice - yes, injustice in the very place where justice should have been administered.
I said to myself, Yes, but God will judge the just and the unjust: for He hath appointed a time for every matter and for every act.
I said to myself, It is for men's sake, that God may show them in their true light, and lead them
to see that they are but beasts. For the fate of men is the fate of beasts: their fate is one and the same. The one dies like the other. One breath is in them all, and man is no way superior to the
beasts. For all is but an illusion. All are on their way to the same place. All sprang from the dust,
and to the dust they shall all return. Who can tell whether the human spirit goes upward and the

spirit of the beast downward to the earth? So O recognised that there is no greater satisfaction for a mn that to be happy in his work - that is his reward; for, as to what is to happen after him- who can give him a glimpse of that? Man's Inhumanity to Man
Once more, I considered all the oppression that goes on under the sun. I saw the tears of the oppressed, who have no one to comfort them- power brutally wielded by the oppressors, and not
a soul to comfort them. Happy, thought I, were the dead who are already dead rather than the
living who are still alive; but happier than either the creature that has never been born, to look upon the evil work that goes on under the sun. The Taint of Jealousy

Then I observed that all the laborious and skilful work of men has its origin and issue in their jealousy of one another. Here is another illusion and a chasing of the wind. The Wisdom of Unambitious Quiet
The fool foldeth his hands, And his own flesh he devoureth.
Better a single handful of quiet, And a chasing of the wind. The Futility and Misery of Loneliness
Here is another of the illusions that I have

observed under the sun. Take, for example, a lonely man, with no one by his side - he has neither son nor brother: yet he toils on endlessly; his eye can never see money enough. "And yet, whom am I toiling for, and beggaring myself to happi- ness? "Here is another illusion, a sorry business indeed.
Two are better than one, for their toil is happily
rewarded. If, for example, one should fall, his comrade helps him to his feet: but woe betide the
man who falls, with nobody to help him up. Again, if two lie together, they get warm: but how can a man

get warm by himself? Again, while a solitary man may be overpowered, two can stand up to an assailant; while a cord that has three strands is not lightly snapped. The Futility of Wisdom - An illustration
A Young man that is poor but wise is better than a foolish old king who can no longer take a warning.
There was one such who passed from prison to the throne, though in the (old king's) reign he had been
born poor; and I obseved that every man alive that walketh under the sun supported his youthful

successor. Endless were the people who looked up to him as leader; and yet in later years their enthusiasm for him had vanished. Here is another illusion and a chasing of the wind. Warnings against Insincerity and Rashness in the Discharge of Religious Duties
walk warily, when you go to the house of God: to participate in the worship with attentive ear is better than the sacrifices offered by fools, who are only versed in the practice of wickedness.
Do not be rash with your tongue, and do not let your feelings hurry you into speech before God: for God is in heaven, while you are on earth; your words ought therefore to be few. For
As dreams proceed from multiplied cares, So the din of fools from multiplied words.
When you make a vow to God, pay it without delay; for fools incur His displeasure. Pay
therefore what you vow. Better not vow than vow

and not pay. Do not allow your tongue to involve you in guilt and punishment; and do not have to explain to the official that it was a case of inadver- tence (on your part). Why should you say things that must provoke God to bring your enterprises
To ruin? for multiplied dreams and words bring multiplied vanities. But hold God in reverence. The Prevalence of Oppression

Do not be astonished when you see a poor man crushed, or right and justice plundered in a pro- vinced; for high officials are perpetually spying upon one another, and over them are others higher still.
It is in every way an advantage to a land to have a king devoted to the cultivation of the soil. The Futility of Wealth
Who loves money can never have money enough, And the lover of riches no increase can satisfy. Here is another illusion.
Increase of wealth bringeth increase in those that consume it: What gain hath is owner save gazing on it with his eyes?
Sweet is the sleep of the toiler, whether he eat much or little; but the satiety of the wealthy man will not let him sleep.
One of the grievous evils that I have observed under the sun is this - wealth hoarded up to its
owner's ruin. The wealth vanishes in some sorry adventure; and so, after becoming a father, he
finds himself with nothing at all. Naked as he came from his mother's womb must he go again, just as he came. For all his toil he can take nothing away with him that he can carry in his
hand. This also is a grievous evil, that he must go away just as he came; and what has he gained
by toiling for the wind? Yes, all his days are spent in darkness and mourning, in deep vexation, sickness and anger.

I claim, as the result of my observation, that it is an excellent and comely thing (for a man) to eat, drink, and enjoy himself amid all his laborious toil under the sun during the days of the brief life
which God gives him: for that is his lot. Yes, when God gives a man wealth and riches and power to enjoy them, to take his share and be happy in
his work - this is a gift of God. Such a man will not think much about the brevity of his life; for his heart is touched by God to a glad response. The Futility of Wealth the Power to Enjoy
One of the vexatious things that I have seen under

the sun to press heavily upon men is this. Take the case of a man to whom God has given wealth, riches, honour, everything heart can desire except the opportunity to enjoy it- that opportunity falling to some stranger. Here is a grieveous and painful illusion.

If a man be the father of a hundred sons, and live for many long years, but without having enjoyed any true satisfaction from his prosperity and without the honour of burial (in the end), then such a man, I maintain, is not so fortunate as an untimely
birth, which, coming as a futility, departs in dark-
ness with its name enveloped in darkness, never having has sight or knowledge of the sunlight. It is this, rather than the other, that enjoys rest.
Though the man should live a thousand years twice over, yet enjoy no experience of happiness, are not both on their way to same place?
The toil of man is all for his mouth, Yet the appetite is unfilled.
What gain hath the wise man more than the fool, Or the poor man who walks through the world with discretion?
Better a glimpse with the eyes Than the roaming of the appetite. Here is another illusion and a chasing of the wind. The Futility of the Struggle with Destiny
The character of what is has already been deter- mined, and the destiny of man is already fore- ordained: he cannot contend with one mightier
than himself. For multiplied words mean but multiplied vanities; and what is man the better?

Who can tell what is good for man during his life- time all the days of the brief and empty life that he passes like a shadow? Who can declare to a man what is to happen after him under the sun? Counsels for Conduct
A fair name is better than precious ointment, And the day of death the day of one's birth.
It is better to go the house of mourning Than to go to the banquetting-house; Inasmuch as that is the end of all men, And the living should lay it to heart.
Vexation is better than laughter; For, when the face is sad, it is well with the heart.
The heart of the wise is in the house of mourning, But the heart of the fool in the house of mirth.
It is better to hear the rebuke of the wise Than to lend one's ears to the song of a fool.
For like crackling of nettles under kettles, Even so is the cackle of fools. Here is another illusion.
Extortion maketh the wise man mad, And a bribe destroyeth the character.
The end of a thing is better than the beginning, And the patience is better than pride.
Do not be hastily vexed in thy temper; Vexation doth lodge in the bosom of fools,
Say not, "Why were the former days better than these?" For such a question is not of wisdom.
As good as an inheritance is wisdom. And gainful to those who behold the sun.
Wisdom defends, even as money defends; But herein is the greater gain of knowledge, That a wisdom is life unto those that possess her.
Consider well the work of God; For who can make straight that which He hath made crooked?
In happy days be happy, and in the day of misfor- tune consider: for God has balanced the one against the other, in order to prevent men from discovering anything of the future. The Folly of Extremes
In the course of my illusory life I have witnessed all sort of things - honest men ruined by their very honesty, and unprincipled men who owe their
long life to their very lack of principle. Do not be over-pious or over-wise: why court destruction?
But neither be over-wicked, nor play the fool:
why die before your time? It is good, while cling- ing to the one, not to relax your grasp of the other; for true religion avoids the excesses of both.
Wisdom is a mightier protector to the wise than
ten men who are in authority over a city. For there is not a single righteous man upon earth- a man who does nothing but good and never falls into sin.
Further, pay no attention to current gossip, in case you may hear that your servant has cursed
you; for your conscience tells you that you too hace cursed others many a time. Woman a Delusion and a Snare
I subjected all this to the test of wisdom. I resolved to acquire wisdom, but she remained remote.
Yes, the essence of things remained remote - deep down in depths unfathomable.
Then, casting about, I gave my mind to the understanding and investigation of wisdom, to search after her for results, and to study the folly

of wickedness and the madness of folly. And a thing that I find to be more bitter than death is woman: for she is a veritable net, with her heart of snares and her hands of fetters. The man who enjoys the favour of God escapes her, but the sinner
is caught by her. Now mark this, says the Speaker. Putting one thing with another in order to arrive
at a conclusion - which, however, I have long and earnestly sought in vain - this at least I have dis- covered: that there is one man in a thousand- that I have discovered - but never a woman in all
that number have I found. This only have I found-mark it well: that men were created upright by God, but they have sought out many contrivances of their own. Reflections upon Despotism
Who is like the wise man? Who is skilled in interpretation? A man's wisdom illumines his face, And a face that is harsh is transfigured.
Obey the king's commands; but, remembering
your oath to God, do not be drawn into hasty action. Leave his presence, and do not embark upon any hurtful course; for a king can do any-
thing he pleases, seeing that his royal word is authorative, and his conduct unchallengeable.
He who keeps the commandment will never come to harm. The wise man knows in his heart
that there is an hour of judgment; for everything has its hour for judgment - and men will be crushed
beneath the weight of calamity. For they are ignorant of the future: who can tell what form it
will take? No man can control the day of his death, any more than he can control or restrain the wind. In war there is no discharge. Wrong will secure no immunity for the wrongdoer.

All this I saw, as I applied my mind to all that goes on under the sun, at a time when men were wielding their power over other men to ruin them. The Futility of Looking for a Moral Order in this World, and there is No Other
Thereafter I saw wicked men borne to the tomb from the holy place - men who usde to go about amid plaudits in the very city where they had so behaved. Here is another illusion.

Because sentence is not swiftly executed upon deeds of wickedness, but a sinner may enjoy a long life thought he do evil a hundred times over, men's hearts swell with the impulse to do evil; though sure I am that it will be well with those who fear God - I mean those
who really reverence Him - but it will not be well with the wicked: his life will be short as a shadow, because he has no reverence for God.

Here is another of the anomalies to be found upon the earth - honest meb who fare as if they had been scoundrels, and scoundrels who fare as if they had been honest men. Here, methought, is another futility. Then I commended mirth - for the only human satisfaction under the sun is to eat, drink, and be merry: these are the things that should accompany men during the days of the laborious life with God has given them under the sun.
When I gave my mind to the study of wisdom and to the observation of the business that is trans- acted in the world - for day and night one never

gets a glimpse of sleep - then I recognised that man is impotent to discover the meaning of all the work of God that goes on under the sun. However laboriously men search, they will never discover it. A wise man may imagine he is on the point of understanding it, but he can never find it out.

For all this I laid to heart, and my heart observed it all, that the just, the wise, and their doings are in the hand of God: no man knows whether it is to be love or hatred. All that lies ahead of them is

vapour, inasmuch as the fate of all is alike - of saint and scoundrel, good and bad, pure and impure, those who do and those who do not practise sacrifice. Good man and sinner fare alike, those who take oaths and those who are afraid to take them.

This is one of the vexing things that go on uni- versally under the sun, that the fate of all is alike. Besides, the human heart is full of evil: all their life long, madness is in men's heart, and thereafter
they join the dead - not a man is left. There is hope for all who are in life- a live dog even is
better than a dead lion- for the living know at least that they have to die, but the dead have no knowledge at all, and no further reward is possible

for them - their very memory is forgotten. Their love, hatred, jealousy, all alike have already vanished, and for all time they have no share in anything that goes on under the sun. The Wisdom of Enjoyment
Go and eat thy bread with joy, And drink thy wine with a merry heart; For what thou doest is God's good pleasure.
At all times let thy raiment be white, And let not oil on thy head be lacking.

Enjoy thy life with the woman thou lovest, All the days of thy fleeting life, Which He hath given thee under the sun. For that is thy reward in thy life of laborious toil under the sun. Do with thy might whatever thou hast in thy power to do; for nothing can be done or devised, known or apprehended, in the under-world to which thou art going. The Element of Chance in Life

Once more: I observed that under the sun it is not the swift that win the race, nor the strong that conquer in battle, neither is wisdom rewarded with bread, nor insight with wealth, nor intellect with practical appreciation; all alike are the victims of

time and chance. Nobody knows his hour. Men are like fish caught in a net, or birds in a trap. Like them men are caught in the meshes in an evil hour, when suddenly it falls upon them. The place of Wisdom in Popular Esteem
The following illustrations of wisdom came under
my notice, and it greatly impressed me. There was a small and thinly garrisoned town, which a power- ful king came and invested by building huge
siege-works against it. But there happened to be in it a man, poor indeed, but endowed with a wisdom which enabled him to save the town. Not a soul,
However, remembered this poor man. So, me- thought, wisdom of a poor man is held in contempt, and his words are not listened to.
Better wise words that are heard in quiet Than the shrieking of one who is lord among fools.
Better is wisdom than weapons of war, For one single blunder may ruin much good. A Topsy-Turvy World
As a deadly fly causes stench in the perfumer's ointment, So a little folly can ruin the rarest wisdom.
The sense of a wise an leads him to the right, But the sense of a fool to the left.
As he goes on his way, a fool showeth his lack of sense; And every one saith of him, "There goes a fool."
If a ruler flare up in a passion against thee, Quit not thy post: for composure Can lull mighty passions to rest.
Under the sun this evil I have seen, As 'twere the blundering order of a Ruler:
The fool is set upon a lofty height, While men of wealth must take a lowly seat.
Slaves have I seen upon horse-back, And princes walking like slaves on the ground.
He that diggeth a pit may fall therein, And a serpent may bite him that breaks through a wall;
He that quarrieth stones may be hurt by them, And the man who cleaves wood is imperilled thereby.
If the iron be blunt, and you whet not its edge, You must use more strength. He succeeds who can claim the advantage of wisdom.
If a serpent bite for lack of enchantment, Then the skillful charmer hath no advantage.
The words of a wise man's mouth win him favour, But a fool by his lips is brought to ruin;
From the first the words of his mouth are folly, And the end of his speech is calamitous madness.
The fool maketh many words. Man knoweth not the future; And what shall happen after him, Who can declare unto him?
The fuss of fools must weary the man Who does not know his way to the town.
Alas for thee, land! when thy king is a youth, And thy princes feast in the early morning;
But hail to thee, land! when thy king is a noble, And thy princes feast at the proper season, Like men and not like sots.
Through idleness the roof sinks in, And through slackness of hands does the house fall a-leaking.
Feasts are made for mirth, And wine cheers the heart of the living, And money answereth all things.
Even in thy thought curse not the king, And curse not the rich in thy sleeping-chamber; For a bird of the air may carry the sound, And the thing that hath wings may declare the matter.
Cast thy bread on the face of the waters, For after many days thou shalt find it.
Give a portion to seven, yea, even to eight, For thou knowest not what evil may come on the land.
When the clouds are filled with rain, They empty it over the earth. If a tree falls northward or southward, In the place where it falls it remains.
He who always is watching the wind Never gets to his sowing. He who always is scanning the clounds Never gets to his reaping.
As thou knowest not what is the way of the wind, Nor how the child grows in the womb of the mother, So knowest thou not how God doth work- He who worketh in all things evermore.

In the morning sow thy seed, And till evening rest not thy hand; For thou knowest not which of the two shall prosper, Or whether both shall be good alike. Rejoice, Young Man, in thy Youth: for the Sorrows of Age are Many and Sure
Sweet is the light, and pleasant it is For the eyes to behold the sun.
For, though a man live many years, All of them filled with gladness, Yet let him remember the days of darkness, For many shall they be. All that cometh is vapour.

Rejoice, young man, in thy youth, And keep thy young heart merry; Walk in the ways of thy heart, And in all that allureth thine eyes. But know that for all these things God will bring thee to judgment.
Put vexation away from thy mind, And banish all gloom from thy body- For youth and life's dawn are illusions-
But keep thy Creator in mind In the days of thy prime: Ere the gloomy days come on, And the years arrive when thou sayest, " No pleasure are they to me:"
The days when the sun grows dark, And the light, and the moon, and the stars, And the rain is followed by clouds,
And the guards of the house fall a-trembling, The mighty men are bent, The grinders cease, being few, Those that look through the windows are darkened,
The doors in the street are shut, When the sound of the mill is low, And the twitter of birds is faint, And the daughters of son are all feeble,

Yea, (the old) are afraid of a height, And the road is for them full of terrors, The almond-tree wears it blossoms, The grasshopper limps along, And the caperberry is powerless; For the man goes his way to his long, long home, And the mourners wander about the streets-
On the day when the silver cord is snapped, ANd the bowl (with the) golden (oil) is broken, And the pitcher is shattered over the spring, And the wheel falls into the cistern broken,
And the dust goes back to the earth as it was, And the breath returns to the God who gave it.
Utterest vanity! The Speaker declareth: All is vanity. A Later Addition in Praise of the Book, and, in general, of Wisdom
The Speaker, besides being wise, further instructed the people in knowledge, weighing and searching it out, and arranging it in the form of copious proverbs.
The Speaker made it his study to devise sayings that were at once true and attractive, and to record them in proper form.

The words of wise men, as collected, are like goads, or like nails driven home; but they are (all) the gift of one shepherd. Beyond these, too, my son, take warning. Books are so many, their making is endless; For this is the essence of all that is human.
For God will bring every work to the judgment That is passed on all secret things, good or bad.

books.name: Song of Solomon
chapters: The most beautiful of love poems, for Solomon. The First Poem
(she says) O for a kiss from your lips, your caresses are better than wine.
Your perfumes are fine in their fragrance, as perfumes poured forth is your name. No wonder the young women love you!
Draw me after you, O let us hasten, O king, bring me into your chamber. In you we'll find joy and be glad, more than wine shall we praise your caresses. Yes, they are right who love you!
I am dark and yet lovely, you girls of Jerusalem, like the dark tents of Kedar, the fine curtains of Solomon.

Don't stare at me strangely because I am dark, it was the sun that scorched me. Once the sons of my mother were angry with me, so they set me to watch the vineyards, but the vineyard that was my own I watched not at all.
O tell me, love of my life, where you rest your flocks, where you rest at noon, for why should I wander in vain and be seen by the flocks of your friends?
(he replies) If you don't know the way, O fairest of women, follow the tracks of my flock, feed your kids where the shepherds are camped. (she says) (he says)
To me you are like, my love, a fine horse in Pharaoh's chariot -
your cheeks all lovely with ornaments, your neck with strings of jewels.
I will make you circlets of gold with pendants of silver.
While the king on his couch is reclining, my fragrant spikenard fills the air.
A scent-bag of myrrh is my loved one, that lies between my breasts.
My love is a cluster of henna in the vineyards of En-gedi.
O how fair you are, my love, how fair, with dove-like eyes!
(she says) O how handsome you are, my beloved! Green grass is our bed,
our house has cedars for beams, and our rafters are fir.
Just a little flower am I, a mere lily of the valleys.
(he says) As a lily among the thorns, so is my darling among the maidens.
(she says) Like a fruit tree in a forest, so is my love among the young men. In his shadow I sit with delight, and his fruit is sweet to my taste.
He brought me to the house of wine, he covered my with his love.
Sustain me with cakes of raisins, refresh me with apples, for Iam faint with love.
His left arm is under my head, and his right arm is around me.
Swear, O girls of Jerusalem, by the gazelles and wild does, don't disturb our love before we are satisfied! The Second Poem
Listen! My lover! See, he comes, leaping the mountains, skipping the hills,
a gazelle, a young stag. See, there he stands in front of our wall, looking through the window, peering though the latice,
my love calls to me: "Come my love, my love, come away.
For see! The winter is past, the rain is over and gone,
the flowers appear in the land, the time for pruning is come, and the turtledove's cooing is heard,
the figs on the fig trees are ripening, the vines are all blossom and fragrance. Arise, my beloved, my fair, come away."
O my dove, from your craggy retreat, from your hiding-place steep, show me your face, let me hear your voice, for your voice is so sweet and your face is so lovely.
Catch the jackals, those little jackals, out to ruin my vineyard, my vineyard in bloom.
My beloved is mine, I am his: he grazes his flock among the lilies.
Till cool grows the day, and the shadows depart, turn, my beloved, swift like the roe or the playful gazelle on the mountains of Bether.
Night after night on my bed I longed for my lover. I longed for him but he was nowhere to be found.
I will rise and go over the city, its streets and its squares - I will seek my beloved! But I could not find him.
I was found by the watchmen who passed on their round of the city. "Tell me," I said, "Have you seen my beloved?"
Scarce had I parted from them when I found my beloved. I held him and would not let go, until I brought him to my mother's home, to the room of my conceiving.
Swear, O girls of Jerusalem, by the gazelles and wild does, don't disturb our love before we are satisfied! The Third Poem
Who is this coming up from the desert like columns of smoke, perfumed with myrrh and frankincense, all scents of the merchant?
Look! It is Solomon's litter, and round it three score valiant men of the valiant of Israel,
each with a sword in his hand, and acquainted with war - every man with a sword at his side ready for the dangers of night.
The king had a palanquin made him of Lebanon wood.
Its posts he had fashioned of silver; its back was of gold. Its seat was of purple: within it was inlaid with ebony.
Come forth and look on the king, daughters of Zion, in the crown he received from his mother the day he was wedded, with gladness of heart.
(he says) How fair, my love, you are, with your dove-like eyes behind your veil, and your hair like a flock of black goats streaming down from mount Gilead;
your teeth like a white flock new shorn just come up from the washing, ranged in a double row, not one of them lacking;
your lips like a scarlet thread and your mouth so lovely; your cheeks like halves of pomegranate, seen through your veil;
your neck like the tower of David, built in circles of stones, hung with a thousand shields, all shields of warriors;
your breasts like a pair of fawns: twin fawns of a gazelle grazing among the lilies.
Till the day grows cool and the shadows depart I will go to your mountains of myrrh, and your hills of frankincense.
All of you is beautiful, my love, beautiful without a flaw.
Come with me from Lebanon, my sweetheart, come from Lebanon, come down from the top of Amana, from the top of Senir and Hermon, from the lions' dens, from the lairs of the leopards.
You have stolen my heart, my sister, my bride, one glance of your eyes has bewitched me, one glint of your necklace. *****

How sweet thy caress, sister bride! How much better than wine thy caresses! Thy lips, O my bride, drip with honey, And milk leith under thy tongue. Thy garments are fragrant as Lebanon; Thy perfumes are sweeter than balsam.
A garden enclosed is my sister, And garden enclosed, a sealed fountain;
Thy shoots are a pomegranate paradise- Choicest of fruits, Henna and spikenard,
Spikenard and saffron, Calamus and cinnamon, With all trees of incense, Aloes and myrrh, With the chief spices all.
Thou art the fount of my garden, A well of fresh water, Like streamlets of Lebanon.
O North wind, awake, And come, thou South, And blow on my garden, That perfume be wafted. "Let my darling come into his garden And taste its choice fruits."

I am come to my garden, my sister; I gather me balsam and myrrh. I have tasted the comb with the honey, And drunk of my wine and my milk. Taste ye, my friends, and drink; Yea, drink yourselves drunken, beloved. In Praise of the Bridegroom The Bride tells her Tale
I slept, but my heart was awake. O hark! my beloved is knocking. "Open to me, my sister, My dove, my beloved, all spotless. For wet is my head with the dew, And my locks with the drops of the night."
"I have laid my garment aside, And why should I don it again? My feet, too, I have washed, And why should I soil them again?"
My love put his hand through the opening, And moved was my heart for him.
So I rose to let in my darling: My hands were dripping with myrrh, With the myrrh that ran over my fingers And on the catch of the bolt.
I opened the door to my love, But my darling had turned and was gone; And the soul of me sank, when he vanished. I sought him, but all in vain: I called, but he gave me no answer.
The watchmen were tramping the city; They met me, they beat me and wounded me. Those who kept watch on the walls Seized the mantle that I had thrown over me.
I adjure you, ye maids of Jerusalem, If so be ye find my beloved, What, then, shall ye tell my beloved? That faint with love am I.
"What is thy darling more than another, O thou of women the fairest? What is thy darling more than another, That thou dost adjure us so?" The Bride's Praise of the Bridegroom
"My beloved is radiant and ruddy, The chief among ten thousand
His head is finest gold; His curls are as black as the raven.
His eyes are like doves Over brooklets of water, Or bathing in milk, As they perch on the brink.
His cheeks are like beds of spices, Like banks of sweet herbs. His lips are like lilies - adrip With their liquid myrrh.
His arms are like tapers of gold That are inlaid with jasper; His belly like ivory wrought And encrusted with sapphires.
His legs are like pillars of marble On sockets of gold. He looks altogether like Lebanon, Grand as the cedars.
Sweetness itself is his mouth; He is all of him lovely. Such is my love and my darling, Ye maids of Jerusalem." Conclusion
"Whither hath gone thy beloved, O fairest of women? Whiter hath turned thy beloved, That with thee we may seek him?"
"My love is gone down to his garden, The terrace of spices, To pasture his flock in his garden, To pasture his flock in his garden And gather the lilies.
My beloved is mine, I am his, 'Mong the lilies he pastures." In Praise of the Bride
Thou art fair, my beloved, as Tirzah, Lovely as Jerusalem, Dread as an army with banners.
Away from me turn those eyes That stir me so strangely, That hair like a flock of goats Streaming down from mount Gilead,
Those teeth like a flock of ewes, Just come up from the washing, Ranged in a double row, Not one of them lacking;
Those temples like pieces of pomegranate Seen through thy veil.
Three score queens had Solomon, Concubines fourscore, And maids without number.
But she, my dove, is but one, She, my stainless, is one. Alone and peerless is she To the mother who bore her. The daughters, at sight of her, praised her; Concubines, queens, sang her praises.
Who is this that looks forth like the dawn, Fair as the moon, Clear as the sun, And dread as an army with banners?
"I went down to the garden of nuts, To see the green shoots in the valley, To see if the vines were in bud, Or the pomegranates yet were in blossom.
Or ever I knew, on his chariot The prince of my people had set me."

Turn thee, turn, maid of Shulem, Turn thee, turn, that we see thee. But what would ye see in the Shulammite? (We would see her dance) the war-dance. In Praise of the Bride as she dances the Sword-Dance
How gracefully thou dost step In thy sandals, thou prince's daughter! The curves of thy thighs are like jewels, wrought by the hands of an artist.
Thy waist is a rounded bowl- Be never the sweet wine wanting. Thy belly a heap of wheat, That is set about with lilies.
Thy breasts like a pair of fawns That are twins of a gazelle,
Thy neck like the ivory tower. Thine eyes are like Heshbon's pools, By the gate of the populous city, Thy nose like the tower of Lebanon That looketh toward Damascus,
Thy head is like Carmel upon thee, Thy flowing locks are as purple: Thy tresses hold captive the king.
How fair, my beloved, art thou! How lovely in love's delights!
Thou art slender and tall as a palm, And thy breasts are like its clusters.
I vowed I would climb this palm, And take hold of the branches thereof. O let thy breasts be as clusters, The smell of thy breath be as apples,
Thy lips be as wine of the best, That goeth down smoothly And glides o'er the lips and the teeth. The Bride's Longing
I am my darling's; He longeth for me.
Come, then, my love, Let us go the to the fields, Let us lodge among the henna.
And early we'll hie to the vineyards, To see if the vine be in bud, If its blossoms have opened, And pomegranates flower; And there my caresses I'll give thee.
The love-apples give forth their scent; At our door are all manner of fruits, Precious fruits, both new and old, That I stored up for thee, my beloved.
O that thou wert my brother, That nursed at the breasts of my mother! If I found thee without, I would kiss thee, Nor fear the contempt of another.
To my mother's house I would bring thee, To the chamber of her that conceived me. Spiced wine would I give thee to drink, A draught of my pomegranate wine. The Incomparable Power of Love

Who is this that comes up from the wilderness, Leaning upon her beloved? "'Neath the apple-tree yonder I woke thee, Just there, where a babe thou wast swaddled- Yes, there - by the mother that bore thee."
"Set me as seal on thy heart Like the ring on thy hand. For love is strong as death, Its passion is fierce as the grave; Its flashes are flashes of fire, Its flames are like the lightning.
No waters can quench it, Nor floods overwhelm it. If a man should give all in his house for it, Utterly scorned would he be. The Bride's Proud Reply to her Brothers
"A sister have we, but she's little- No breasts hath she yet. What then shall we do with our sister, The day she is spoken for?
If she should prove a wall, We will build her a turret of silver. But if she should prove a door, We will fence her with boards of cedar."
"I was a wall, My breasts were the turrets; And he, as he looked at me, Won me my happiness." The Two Vineyards
Solomon had a vineyard at Baal-hamon- He let out the vineyard to keepers; And each man brought in for the fruit thereof One thousand shekels of silver.
I, too, have a vineyard, mine own: I leave thee the thousand, O Solomon; Yea, and two hundred besides For the men who kept watch on the fruit thereof, Conclusion
"O thou that hauntest the gardens, My comrades are all attention: Come, let us hear thy voice."
"Hasten, beloved, And flee like the roe Or the playful gazelle On the mountains of spices."

books.name: Isaiah
chapters: The vision of Judah and Jerusalem, as seen by Isaiah the son of Amoz, in the days of Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah, kings of Judah. The prophet's lament over the unfaithfulness of the people to their God
Hear, you heavens! Listen, earth, for the Lord* is the speaker. "I raised my childen and brought them up, but they have rebelled against me.
Even an ox knows its owner, and a donkey its master's manger; but Israel does not know, my people are thoughtless."
Sinful nation! A people laden with guilt, a brood of evildoers, children all corrupt, they have forsaken the Lord*, spurned the Holy One of Israel, turned their backs on him.
Why would you invite more beatings by straying yet further from him? Your whole head is sick, your heart is all diseased,
from the sole of the foot to the head there is no health at all, nothing but bruises and welts and wounds that are raw and bleeding, all unpressed and unbandaged, all unsoftened with oil.
Your land is a desolation, your cities are burned with fire, the fields before your eyes are being devoured by foreigners.
And the daughter of Zion is left all alone like a booth in a vineyard, a lodge in a cucumber field, like a city under siege.
Were it not that the Lord* of Hosts had spared a few of us, we would be like Sodom, or another Gomorrah. The futility of a merely ceremonial worship
Attend to the Lord*'s message, you rulers of Sodom! Hear what our God has to teach you, you folk of Gomorrah.
"Why do you think I care," says the Lord*, "for your countless sacrifices? I am sick of burnt offerings of rams and the fat of fed steers, blood of bulls, lambs, or goats, is no pleasure to me.
When you come before me in worship, who has asked you for these things? Trample my courts no more,
bring offerings no more. Your offerings are meaningless, their incense disgusts me. The new moon and Sabbath, the call to assembly, your sinful ceremonies I cannot endure.
I loathe your new moon festivals, your annual assemblies. They weigh me down, I am tired of the burden.
So when you lift up your hands, I will turn away my eyes; when you make your many prayers, I will not listen to you. Your hands are full of blood:
wash, and make youself clean. Banish out of my sight the wicked things that you do. Cease to do evil,
learn to do good. Seek justice, help the oppressed, uphold the rights of the orphan, defend the cause of the widow." The great alternatives
"Come," says the Lord*, "and now let us reason together. Your sins, though like scarlet, may yet become white as the snow. Though they be crimson-red, they may yet be as wool.
If you are obedient and willing, you will eat the good of the land;
but if you refuse and rebel, by the sword you will be eaten, for the mouth of the Lord* has spoken it." Zion's present shame and future glory
Alas! How the once faithful city has become a prostitute. She who was once full of justice, where righteousness resided, \ is now haunted by murderers.
Your silver is now become dross, and your wine mixed with water.
Your rulers are grown to be rebels, the companions of thieves - every one of them fond of their bribe, hunting ever for gifts, caring nothing for the rights of the orphan or the cause of the widow.
This then says the Lord, God* of Hosts, the Mighty One of Israel: "I will get satisfaction from my foes, wreck vengeance on my enemies.
I will turn my hand against you, I will melt you down and skim off your slag, I will remove your impurities.
I will give you good judges again counsellors as you used to have, and then you will called the city of justice, the faithful city.
Zion will be ransomed by justice, and by righteousness her people.
But rebels and sinners will be crushed, and those who abandon the Lord* will perish. The heathen cult and its doom
The sacred oaks you delight in will bring you shame, the gardens you worship in will make you blush.
Like a tree with withered leaves you will be - like a waterless garden.

The strong will become like tinder, their evil deeds the spark, and both will burn together and no one will quench the flame. Jerusalem the centre of blessing to the world; arbitration, disarmament, and international peace Marker
The message of Isaiah the son of Amoz: his vision of Judah and Jerusalem.
In the after-time it will be that the mountain of the Lord* will be set at the head of the mountains, and exalted above the hills. All the peoples will thither stream,

many nations will go and say, "Come, let us go to the mount of the Lord*, to the house of the God of Jacob, so that he in his ways may instruct us, and that we in his paths may walk. For instruction proceeds from Zion, the word of the Lord* from Jerusalem."

He will judge the disputes of the peoples, and for manifold nations so justly will arbitrate that their swords they will beat into ploughshares, and to pruning-hooks their spears. Nation will not lift up sword against nation, and war they will learn no more.
O household of Jacob, come, let us walk in the light of the Lord*. Judgment upon the wealth and pride of Judah The Lord's judgement-day

(Get you into the caves of the rocks, and hide in the holes of the ground, from the Lord*'s dread face, when in glory majestic he rises with terror to smile the earth.) * For he has forsaken his people, the household of Jacob. Filled is his land from the east with diviners, with soothsayers like to the Philistines, sorcerers, children of foreigners.
Filled in his land full of silver and gold, and his treasure is endless. Filled in his land full of horses, his chariots are endless.
Filled is his land full of idols, he worships the work of his hands, the things that his fingers have made.
(Get you into the rocks, and hide in the ground, from the Lord*'s dread presence and glory majestic.) *
So the pride of men will be humbled, laid low will man's loftiness be, and that day will the Lord* alone be exalted.
For a day of the Lord* is coming upon all that is haughty and proud, upon all that is lofty and high-
upon cedars of Lebanon all, and oaks of Bashan all,
upon all the great mountains and all the high hills,
upon all the proud towers, all fortified walls,
upon all ships of Tarshish and all gallant craft.
Then the pride of men will be humbled, laid low will man's loftiness be, and that day will the Lord* alone be exalted.
The Idols will all of them vanish.
Get you into the caves of the rocks, and the holes of the ground from the Lord*'s dread face, when in glory majestic he rises with terror to smile the earth. *
That day will men cast away to the moles and to the bats their idols of silver and gold, which they made for themselves to worship;
And into the caves of the rocks they will get them, and into the rents of the cliffs, from the Lord*'s dread face, when in glory majestic he rises-with terror to smite the earth. *
Oh cease your trust in man, in whose nostrils is but a breath. Of what account is he? A reign of anarchy
Behold, the Lord the Lord* of Hosts take staff and stay from Jerusalem and Judah-
soldiers and Warriors, judges and prophets,
deviners and elders, and captains of fifty, men of distinction, and men of sage counsel, cunning magicians and skilful enchanters.
And boys I will give them for princes, and men of caprice will rule over them.
The people will play the tyrant, each man over his neighbour. The young will be rude to the aged, the man of low rank to the high.
One will take hold of his fellow, and say to him: "Your family has a robe; come, and do you be our ruler, and take this ruin in hand."
Then will the other protest, "No, truly: I* cannot dress your wounds. In my own house there is no bread, nor yet is there a robe. You will not thrust upon me* The leadership of the people."
Jerusalem is stumbling to ruin, and Judah must surely fall; for by word and by deed they defy the Lord*, provoking those glorious eyes of his.
Their respecting of persons is witness against them; like Sodom they publish their sin undisguisedly. Woe unto them! They have wrought their own ruin.
Happy righteous! For well they will fare, they will reap the fruit of their doings.
But woe to the wicked! For ill they will fare, their deeds will be recompensed unto them.
My people are cruelly governed, extortions rule over them. O My people, your leaders mislead, and confuse the way you should go.
The Lord* is taking his place for the trial, he stands to judge his people.
The Lord* summons to judgment the elders and princes of his people. "Yes, you* have devoured the vineyard, the spoil of the poor is in your houses.
What mean you by crushing my people and grinding the faces of the poor?" says the Lord God* of Hosts. The doom of the haughty women
Moreover the Lord* said: "Because Zion's daughters are haughty, walking with heads held high, and eyes for ever ogling, with dainty little steps, and anklets ever jingling,
the Lord will smite with a scab the head of the daughters of Zion, and their shame will the Lord* lay bare.
That day the Lord will remove the finery of the anklets, the net-bands and the moons,
the ear-drops and bracelets and veils,
the head-dresses, armlets, and sashes, the perfume-boxes and amulets,
the signet-rings and the nose-rings,
the state-gowns, the mantles, the shawls, and the satchels,
the gauzes and linens, the turbans and veils.
Sweet scents will give place unto stench, and the girdle be changed for a rope. And well-set hair will be bald, and for elegant robe will be sackcloth, for beauty the brand (of a slave).
Your liegemen will fall by the sword, and your mighty men in the battle.
Her gates will lament and mourn, as she lies despoiled on the ground.
That day will seven women take hold of one man and say, "Our own bread will we eat, and our rainment will we wear; but oh, let us bear your name, and take our reproach away." Zion's final glory
In that day will the wild vegetation be glorious and fair, and the fruit of the tilled land majestic and comely for Israel's sons who escape.
And those who remain in Zion, and those who are left in Jerusalem, will be called by the name of holy - all who stand in the book of life.
When the Lord will have washed away the filth of the daughters of Zion, and rinsed away from her midst the blood-stains of Jerusalem, by means of the blast of judgment, the blast of extermination,
then will the Lord* come, and o'er the whole site of mount Zion and over all the glory
will cover and canopy be - a shade by day from the heat, a refuge and shelter from storm and rain. The vineyard with the wild grapes The song of the vineyard
A song will I sing of my friend, a love-song touching his vineyard. A vineyard belonged to my friend, on a fertile hill-top he had set it.

He dug it and cleared it of stones, he planted in it choice vines. He built in the middle a watchtower, hewed a pit for pressing the grapes. Then he looked for a yield of good grapes, but the grapes that it yielded were wild.
Now judge, you who dwell in Jerusalem, and you who are freemen of Judah, judge between me and my vineyard.
What more could I do for my vineyard that I had neglected to do? And why, when I looked for good grapes, did it yield only grapes that were wild?
So now let me give you to know what I purpose to do my vineyard. I will tear off its hedge, that the beasts may devour it; I will break through its wall, that they trample it down.
I will make it waste, all unpruned and unweeded, with thorns and with briars overgrown it will be, and the clouds I will order to withhold from it rain.

For the vineyard of the Lord* of Hosts is the household of Israel, the freeman of Judah his cherished plantation. But instead of the justice he looked for was bloodshed, instead of the right was the cry (of the wronged). The national sins: woe!
Woe unto you who join house unto house and who add one field to another, till no one has room but you, and you settle the land by yourselves.
The Lord* of Hosts in my ear has whispered, surely many a great fine house will be desolate and empty,
for ten acres of vineyard will yield but eight gallons, and the harvest will be but one tenth of the seed.
Woe unto them who rise early to give themselves to drink, and to those who sit late in the evening, inflaming themselves with wine;
whose banquets of wine are enlivened with lute, harp, timbrel, and flute; but all blind to the work of the Lord*, they see not the things he is doing.
Therefore all unaware will my people be swept into exile afar - their nobleman dying of hunger, their populace parched with thirst.
Therefore Sheol with ravenous throat opens wide her jaws without measure. And down will her splendour go, and her noisy tumultuous rabble, with all who in her were exultant.
Thus through judgment the Lord* of Hosts is exalted, the holy God shows himself holy by righteousness.
And there will lambs graze as at pasture, and fatlings will feed in her ruins.
Woe unto those who draw penalty on by their sin, as by stout wagon-ropes drawn by oxen;
who say, "Let him haste, let him act with speed, in order that we may see it; let the purpose of Israel's Holy One come so near that we recognize it."
Woe unto those who call evil good, and good evil; to those who turn light into darkness, and darkness to light; to those who turn sweet into bitter, and bitter to sweet.
Woe unto those who esteem themselves wise, and who fancy themselves to be prudent.
Woe unto those who are valiant in wine-drinking, warriors brave at the mingling of drink;
whom a bribe will induce to acquit the guilty, and innocent men to deprive of their rights.

As fire licks up the stubble, and hay is shrivelled in flame, so their root will turn to rottenness, and their blossom go up in dust; because they rejected the Lord*'s instruction, and the message of Israel's Holy One scorned. A foreign army is coming

So against his people his anger was kindled, against them he stretched forth his hand and he smote them; the mountains shook, and the dead lay like refuse about the streets. For all this his anger is not turned back, but his hand is stretched out still.*
To a far-distant nation he raises his signal, and whistles for them from the end of the earth. See! Hastily, swiftly they come -
none weary, none stumbling among them, unsleeping and slumbering never: the band of their loins never loosed, the thong of their shoes never torn.
Their arrows are sharp, and their bows are all bent: the hoofs of their horses are counted as flint, and their wheels as the whirlwind.
Their roar is like that of a lioness, and like the young lions they roar, growling and seizing the prey, and bearing it far beyond rescue.
That day they will roar over him, with a roar like the roar of the sea: when he looks on the earth, behold! Darkness, the light has grown dark in the clouds. The prophet's call
In the year that King Uzi died, I had a vision of the Lord seated on a high and lofty throne, and
the skirts if his robe filled the Temple. Before him were standing seraphs, each with six wings - two for covering the face, two the loins, and two
to fly with; and thus they kept calling to one another: "Holy, holy, holy, is the Lord* of Hosts; the whole earth is filled with his glory."
At the sound of their calling the foundations of threshold shook, and the House began to fill
with smoke. Then I said "Woe is me, for I am undone; for a man of unclean lips am I, and I dwell in a nation of unclean lips; and yet my eyes have seen the king, the Lord* of Hosts."
Then one of the seraphs flew to me with a live coal in his hand, which with tongs he had taken
from off the altar. With this he touched my mouth, and said, "See, this has touched your lips: your guilt is past and your sin forgiven."
Then I heard the voice of the Lord, saying, "Whom should I send? Who will go for us?" And I said, "Here am I, send me."
Then he said, "Go and say to this people 'Hear ever, but understand never; see ever, but comprehend never.'
Make the people's minds dull, block their ears and cover their eyes, lest they see with their eyes, lest they hear with their ears, and their minds understand, and their health come again."
Then I said, "Till cities lie wasted, with not an inhabitant left; till houses hold men no more, and the land is left a desolation;
till the Lord* removes men afar and wide tracts of the land lie forsaken.

And should there be in it a tenth still left, that too, in its turn, must be given to the fire, like the stump of an oak or a terebinth felled." The crisis Created by the menace to Judah in 735 B.C. The prophet's word to the terrified king

In the days Ahaz, the son of Jotham, the son of Uzziah, Rezin King of Aram, and Pekah, the son of Remaliah, King of Israel, marched against Jerusalem to attack it, but they were unable to develop an actual assault upon it.
When news reached the Court that the Aramean army was on Ephraimite soil, the heart of Ahaz and his people shook like forest-trees before the
wind. Then the Lord* said to Isaiah, "Go out - you and your son Shear-yashub - to meet Ahaz at the end of the conduit of the upper pool on the

Fuller's Field Road, and say to him: be careful to keep calm. Be not faint-hearted or afraid of this pair of fire-brands that are nothing but smoking stumps. Be not afraid of the fierce anger of Rezin
and Aram and the son of Remaliah. Aram and Ephraim with the son of Remaliah have indeed
plotted your ruin: their purpose is to invade Judah, and, after reducing her straits, to break into (Jerusalem); then, having overpowered her, they intend to set the son of Tabeel on the throne.
But thus says the Lord the God*: 'This thing will not succeed,
for the head of Aram is Damascus, and the head of Damascus is Rezin;
the head of Ephraim is Samaria, and the head of Samaria is the son of Remaliah. Your faith must firmly hold, if you would yourselves be held.'" The great refusal and the sign
